forecasts.reasoning: The question resolves YES if there is EVEN ONE DAY out of the 42-day window (May 20 to June 30) where neither the NYT 'todayspaper' summary nor the WSJ front page contains the words 'tariff' or 'tariffs'. While trade policy is a dominant news topic under the current administration, mathematical probability strongly favors an omission over a full 6-week continuous streak. Assuming even a highly inflated 95% chance of the word appearing on at least one paper's front page index every single day, the odds of a 42-day unbroken streak are only ~0.11 (0.95^42). Furthermore, WSJ does not publish a Sunday edition, meaning on the 6 Sundays during this period, the streak rests entirely on the NYT index. News cycles inevitably shift towards other crises, domestic events, weekend features, or international news that push specific trade terminology out of the summaries on slow days. Therefore, the likelihood of a single day omitting the word 'tariff' is very high.

The resolution requires just a SINGLE DAY within a 42-day window (May 20 to June 30) where the word "tariff" or "tariffs" does not appear in the digital front page headlines/blurbs of EITHER the NYT's Today's Paper or WSJ's Print Edition Front Page. For this to resolve NO, the word must appear on at least one of these two pages every single day without interruption. 

Given the nature of news cycles—which are currently also heavily saturated with other geopolitical events (Iran conflicts, Lebanon, etc.)—the likelihood that a specific policy tool ("tariff") is mentioned in a headline or summary blurb every single day for six weeks is quite low. Furthermore, the WSJ does not publish a Sunday print edition, meaning on the six Sundays within the resolution window, a "gap day" depends entirely on the New York Times failing to use the word in its headlines. Over a 42-day period, the mathematical probability of a single joint "gap day" approaches 90%, even if the base rate of the word's appearance on any given day is individually high (e.g., if NYT has it 80% of days and WSJ 70% of days, the chance of a 42-day streak without a joint miss is less than 15%). Without evidence of a hardcoded daily "Tariff Tracker," a Yes resolution is highly probable.
